title: Work Experience IIT
content:
## Work Experience: Research Intern — Indian Institute of Technology (IIT) Delhi

**Role:** Research Intern  
**Organization:** Indian Institute of Technology (IIT) Delhi  
**Dates:** January 2024 – May 2024  
**Supervisors:** Dr. Lalan Kumar (IIT Delhi), Prof. Amit Rathod (VGEC)

### Overview
Spent 5 months at IIT Delhi as a research intern working on a wearable gesture recognition system as a Bachelor's final year project. Built an air-writing recognition device: a wristband with IMU sensors that captures hand movements while users write characters in the air, then classifies them into **62 classes** (**A–Z uppercase, a–z lowercase, 0–9**) using deep learning.

Owned the full stack: **hardware assembly** (ESP32 + MPU6050 sensor wired via **I2C**, communicating over **Wi-Fi**), **custom dataset collection**, **data preprocessing in Python**, and **ML model development**. The core research effort focused on systematically comparing architectures by building and evaluating **1,000+ model configurations** across **Dense, 1D CNN, LSTM, BiLSTM, and hybrid combinations** using **TensorFlow/Keras**, ultimately selecting a **CNN-BiLSTM hybrid**.

Collaborated during model optimization with researchers from **IISc Bangalore** and a post-doctoral researcher from **Howard University**, refining architecture search strategy and evaluation methodology.

### Key Contributions
- Designed and built the wearable hardware prototype: **ESP32 + MPU6050 IMU**, **C++ firmware**, **Wi-Fi data streaming**
- Collected a structured dataset: **20 participants × 62 gestures × 10 repetitions = 12,400 labeled samples**
- Implemented an end-to-end Python pipeline: **sensor data ingestion → Z-score normalization → model training → real-time inference**
- Systematically tested **1,000+** model configurations across **5 architecture families**, identifying **CNN-BiLSTM** as optimal via empirical hyperparameter search
- Applied rigorous evaluation:
  - **Leave-one-subject-out cross-validation** for **user-independent** testing
  - **Per-subject splits** for **user-dependent** testing
- Achieved results on 62-class classification with the selected CNN-BiLSTM hybrid:
  - **75% user-dependent accuracy**
  - **57% user-independent accuracy**
- Wrote a research paper documenting methodology and results

### Tech Stack
**Python, TensorFlow, Keras, NumPy, C++ (Arduino), ESP32, MPU6050, Wi-Fi, I2C protocol**

### What I Learned
Gained deep exposure to research methodology: designing controlled experiments, collecting clean datasets, running systematic architecture searches, and understanding the difference between inflated accuracy (random splits) and honest accuracy (**leave-one-subject-out**). Learned to evaluate models rigorously and communicate technical trade-offs clearly through collaboration with researchers from IISc Bangalore and Howard University.

title: Work Experience STTL
content:
## Work Experience: AI/ML Developer — Silver Touch Technologies Ltd (June 2024 – July 2025)

### Context
Joined Silver Touch Technologies Ltd immediately after an IIT Delhi internship. Before starting, worked with Prof. Mayank Singh at IIT Gandhinagar, gaining foundational knowledge in embedding models and retrieval systems relevant to subsequent production work.

### Primary Responsibilities & Scope
- Built and optimized a production RAG chatbot platform serving **thousands of clients**, handling **50,000+ queries/month**, and running **10+ chatbot instances simultaneously**.
- Built an **on-premise OCR and document processing pipeline** for a client with strict **data sovereignty** constraints.

### RAG Chatbot Platform (Production)
**Initial state / problems:**
- LLM **Llama-2-7b** loaded directly in application code (not a separate service).
- Vector database was **FAISS**, delivering **45% MRR** and causing major latency bottlenecks.

**Model serving overhaul:**
- Separated the LLM into a standalone service using **VLLM**, creating an API layer.
- Outcomes: decoupled model from application, enabled independent scaling, model swaps without redeployment, and improved GPU utilization.

**Vector database evaluation (production-scale: millions of vectors):**
- Led evaluation of **Milvus** while a colleague evaluated **Qdrant**.
- Benchmark results on production data: **Milvus 95% MRR**, **Qdrant 85% MRR**, **FAISS 45% MRR**.
- Decision: selected **Milvus** for production due to retrieval quality and scale handling.

**Embedding model experimentation & retrieval logic:**
- MRR gains attributed not only to vector DB change, but also to systematic embedding experiments and custom retrieval logic.
- Evaluated: **BGE (small/base/large)**, **Stella (small/large)**, **SPLADE (sparse embeddings)**.
- Final approach: **BGE-M3 + reranker**, plus **custom query preprocessing** and reranking logic.
- Result: improved MRR from **45% to 95%**.

**Latency optimization:**
- Reduced response time from **16s → 8s** by removing FAISS bottleneck, implementing query preprocessing with **intent identification**, and optimizing the retrieval pipeline.

### On-Premise OCR & Document Processing (Sensitive Documents)
**Constraints & environment:**
- Documents included sensitive government IDs: **Aadhaar numbers, PAN cards, GST numbers, addresses**, and other identifiers.
- Input: **200+ page PDFs**.
- Hard requirement: **fully on-premise** (no cloud APIs, no external LLMs; no data leaves client servers).
- Client purchased a dedicated **250GB server**.

**Hybrid OCR approach:**
- Found no single open-source OCR met requirements.
- Testing outcome:
  - **DocLink**: accurate spelling/character recognition.
  - **DocTR**: better markdown structure and layout preservation.
- Solution: run **both OCRs per page**, then use **Llama-3.1-Nemotron-70b-Reward** hosted on the client’s on-premise server to merge outputs (spelling accuracy from DocLink + structure from DocTR).
- Result: hybrid OCR quality better than either OCR alone.

**Entity extraction:**
- Built entity extraction on top of OCR to identify/extract structured entities (Aadhaar number, PAN number, GST number, addresses, other identifiers) from scanned PDFs.
- Achieved **~90% accuracy** on real-world documents with varying quality/formatting.

**Output formats:**
- Converted extracted content to:
  - **Markdown** (preserving headings, tables, lists and document structure)
  - **JSON** (structured entity data)

### Key Contributions (Quantified)
- Rebuilt RAG infrastructure: **FAISS → Milvus**, in-code model → **VLLM-served API**, achieving **111% MRR improvement (45% → 95%)**.
- Benchmarked and selected **Milvus over Qdrant** on production-scale data.
- Tested **6+ embedding models** and implemented custom retrieval with **BGE-M3 + reranker**.
- Cut response latency **50% (16s → 8s)**.
- Built hybrid OCR system: **DocLink + DocTR + on-premise LLM** merge.
- Designed government-ID entity extraction pipeline with **~90% accuracy**.
- Production deployment: **50K+ queries/month**, **10+ concurrent instances**, **thousands of clients**.

### Tech Stack
**Python, FastAPI, VLLM, Llama-2-7b, Llama-3.1-Nemotron-70b, Milvus, FAISS (legacy), Qdrant (evaluated), BGE-M3, BGE (small/base/large), Stella, SPLADE, DocTR, DocLink, on-premise GPU servers**

### What I Learned
- Production-scale AI engineering: bridging notebook prototypes to reliable systems at **50K+ queries/month**.
- Running head-to-head benchmarks on production data with business-impacting decisions (Milvus vs Qdrant).
- Combining imperfect tools (hybrid OCR) under strict on-prem constraints.
- Practical considerations: model selection, GPU memory management, and inference optimization without cloud support.

title: Work Experience Vivansh
content:
## Work Experience: AI Engineer — Vivansh Infotech (August 2025 – Present)

Joined **Vivansh Infotech** as an **AI Engineer** responsible for building AI-powered features for the company’s enterprise **EHS (Environmental Health & Safety) platform**—a **multi-tenant** system serving **500+ concurrent users** across organizations managing **safety compliance, training, and incident reporting**. In under a year, shipped **three production AI systems**, each addressing a core platform problem.

### Project 1: Multi-Tenancy Permission-Aware RAG Chatbot (Agentic Upgrade)
Built a **permission-aware RAG chatbot** where each user has a unique permission profile (**250+ permissions**) and different course assignments (**750+ courses**). Key challenge: two users asking the same question may require different answers due to access control, making traditional caching ineffective. The system has since been **upgraded to a fully agentic architecture** using **LangChain** (tool-calling agents over the platform's APIs) and **LangGraph** (stateful graph that orchestrates the 2-stage retrieval + permission-filtering + tool-use loop) so the bot can autonomously fetch live data and trigger workflows end-to-end while still enforcing 250+ permissions per query.

- **Architecture:** 2-stage retrieval.
  - **Stage 1 (Permission filtering):** Query filtered through a dedicated **permission index (Milvus)**, cross-referenced with user permissions in **PostgreSQL**. Used **GPT-4o** to determine access level and rewrite the query using conversation context.
  - **Stage 2 (Content retrieval):** Retrieve from content index (**10,000+ chunks** from website pages, knowledge base, courses, external content) with **metadata-based course filtering**.
- **Context handling:** Injects page-aware context using the user’s current **URL**; maintains continuity across the last **5 turns**, re-validating permissions every turn.
- **Content ingestion:** Built a **Selenium crawler** that recognizes dynamic UI elements (**tabs, dropdowns, accordions**) to capture hidden content.
- **Multilingual approach:** Implemented a **translate-bridge** pipeline (any language → English → RAG pipeline → English response → target language) that **outperformed native multilingual models**.
- **Production metrics:** ~**8s** response time, **80% MRR**, ~**1,000 queries/day**, **500+ concurrent users**, multilingual support.
- **Stack:** Python, FastAPI, **LangChain (agents + tool calling)**, **LangGraph (stateful agent orchestration)**, Milvus (**2 indexes**), OpenAI **text-embedding-3-large**, **BGE-M3**, **GPT-4o**, PostgreSQL, Selenium, Google Translate, AWS EC2.

### Project 2: AI-Powered Form Builder
Addressed a bottleneck in the platform’s form management portal where admins manually recreated paper-based PDF forms (safety inspections, compliance checklists, incident reports) via drag-and-drop—taking **1+ hour per form** and scaling poorly across tenants.

- **Two-part system:**
  - **Part 1 — PDF Converter:** Upload any PDF form and generate a complete digital replica in ~**20 seconds** (~**7K tokens**). Supports **bulk upload of 100+ PDFs**, processing all in **5–10 minutes**.
  - **Part 2 — Conversational AI Builder:** For net-new forms, takes a natural-language description and runs a multi-step refinement flow: **title/type/category generation → clarifying questions → markdown blueprint → iterative refinement → final form**.
- **Core design decision:** Created an intermediate **JSON schema** (validated with **Pydantic**) that LLMs can generate reliably, then implemented a deterministic conversion layer to transform it into the platform’s deeply nested native form JSON.
- **Multi-model setup:** **GPT-4o** for PDF analysis/metadata; **Qwen3-Coder-480B** (via Together AI) for blueprint generation (outperformed GPT-4o in testing); **GPT-4o-mini** for final JSON conversion.
- **Production metrics:** **200+** forms generated via PDF converter in **month 1**; **15+** via AI builder; both in production.
- **Stack:** Python, FastAPI, GPT-4o, GPT-4o-mini, Qwen3-Coder-480B (Together AI), Pydantic.

### Project 3: AI-Powered Course Builder (Training Video Course Pipeline)
Automated production of training video courses (narrated lessons, background music, text overlays, transitions, sequenced clips). Manual production took ~**1 week per course**; built an end-to-end AI pipeline generating complete courses in ~**10 minutes**.

- **Workflow (3 phases):** Upload course document → AI generates outline for review → AI generates lessons in parallel → user previews → final serverless rendering.
- **Video retrieval & selection engine:**
  - Preprocessed **10,000+ source videos** into **10-second clips**.
  - Analyzed each clip with **Gemini 2.5 Pro** for visual understanding.
  - Embedded with **Cohere embed-english-v3.0** and indexed in **Milvus**.
  - At generation time, RAG retrieves candidate clips; **Gemini** selects/arranges clips and generates narration scripts.
  - Implemented a **reordering algorithm** to ensure visual–audio synchronization.
- **Rendering:** All **FFmpeg** rendering (cut/merge/overlays/audio mixing/transitions) runs on **AWS Lambda** to prevent server overload.
- **Production metrics:** **99% reduction** in production time (**1 week → ~10 minutes**), enabling scaled content production without headcount increase.
- **Stack:** Python, FastAPI, Milvus, Cohere (**embed-english-v3.0 + rerank-v4.0-pro**), Gemini 2.5 Pro, ElevenLabs, FFmpeg, AWS Lambda, S3, SAM CLI.

### Key Contributions at Vivansh Infotech
- Shipped **3 production AI systems** in under a year, each solving a distinct EHS platform problem.
- Designed permission-aware RAG architecture handling **250+ permissions** and **750+ courses** per query.
- Built form generation that reduced **1+ hour** of manual work to **~20 seconds** (**200+ forms** in the first month).
- Created video course pipeline replacing **1-week** manual production with **~10-minute** AI generation.
- Architected **multi-model systems** (GPT-4o, Qwen3-Coder, Gemini, Cohere) based on empirical testing.
- All three systems are in production serving real users on the client’s enterprise platform.

### Overall Tech Stack (Vivansh Infotech)
Python, FastAPI, **LangChain**, **LangGraph**, GPT-4o, GPT-4o-mini, Gemini 2.5 Pro, Qwen3-Coder-480B, Cohere, ElevenLabs, Milvus, PostgreSQL, OpenAI Embeddings, BGE-M3, Selenium, FFmpeg, AWS (EC2, Lambda, S3, SAM CLI), Pydantic, Google Translate.

### What I Learned (Vivansh Infotech)
Focused on shipping production AI systems quickly by understanding client workflows, identifying high-leverage AI opportunities, and building end-to-end solutions used daily. Reinforced the importance of **multi-model architecture** (no single model is best at everything) and the value of clean interfaces between components. The form builder work highlighted how the right abstraction—an intermediate **Pydantic-validated JSON**—can make an unreliable LLM task reliable.

title: Project: Air Writing Recognition System
content:
## Project: Air Writing Recognition System (IIT Delhi Research Internship + Bachelor’s Final Year Project, Jan–May 2024)

**Air Writing Recognition System** — wearable device that recognizes characters written in the air using **IMU sensors (MPU6050)** and **deep learning (CNN-BiLSTM)**. Built as a research internship project at **IIT Delhi, Department of Electrical Engineering** (Jan–May 2024). **Supervising Faculty:** Dr. Lalan Kumar (IIT Delhi), Prof. Amit Rathod (Vishwakarma Government Engineering College).

### Goal / Problem
Air writing enables assistive tech, AR/VR interfaces, and touchless interaction, but faces accuracy vs portability constraints:
- **Computer vision**: high accuracy but needs cameras, line-of-sight, controlled environments.
- **EEG**: low accuracy.
- **EMG**: better than EEG but requires electrode placement and still underperforms motion-based methods.
- **IMU (accelerometer/gyroscope)**: portable and environment-independent; key open challenge is **cross-user generalization** due to differences in writing speed, orientation, stroke order, amplitude.

### End-to-End Solution
Designed and implemented a full pipeline spanning **custom wearable hardware**, **data collection**, **preprocessing**, and **real-time classification**:
- Users wear an **ESP32 + MPU6050** wristband and write characters naturally in the air (no strict orientation, no stroke segmentation, no external infrastructure).
- ESP32 streams sensor data to a **Python backend** over **Wi‑Fi**.
- Backend applies **Z-score normalization** and feeds **150-timestep sequences** to a trained **CNN-BiLSTM** model for **62-class** prediction.

### Hardware & Streaming Architecture
**Wristband components (I2C):**
- **ESP32 microcontroller** (dual-core, Wi‑Fi + Bluetooth): samples at **60Hz**, buffers data, and transmits wirelessly without timing conflicts (not guaranteed by single-core alternatives like ESP8266/NodeMCU).
- **MPU6050 6-axis IMU**: 3-axis accelerometer (m/s²) + 3-axis gyroscope (deg/s); captures full hand kinematics.

**Per-gesture data:**
- **2.5 seconds × 60Hz = 150 timesteps**
- **150 timesteps × 6 channels = 900 raw data points per gesture**

**Wi‑Fi vs Bluetooth (implementation decision):**
- Initial Bluetooth via **pyblue** was **Linux-only**.
- Switched to **Wi‑Fi** to remove OS restrictions (Windows/macOS/Linux), provide higher bandwidth for continuous **60Hz** streaming, and better indoor range.

### Dataset & Data Collection
Structured data collection with real-world writing variation:
- **Participants:** 20
- **Classes:** 62 (A–Z, a–z, 0–9)
- **Repetitions:** 10 per gesture per participant
- **Total samples:** 20 × 62 × 10 = **12,400 labeled samples**
- **Per-sample format:** 150 timesteps × 6 channels = **900 values**, stored as **JSON**
- **Gesture duration:** standardized at **2.5 seconds**

### Preprocessing
**Z-score normalization** applied independently per sensor channel across the dataset:

```text
z = (x - μ) / σ
```

Rationale:
- Different sensor axes/units have different scales (gravity effects on acceleration; gyro vs accel units).
- Prevents models from overweighting high-variance channels.
- Improved stability/convergence; **min-max scaling tested but performed worse**.

**Final tensor:** **(12,400 samples, 150 timesteps, 6 channels)** as NumPy arrays.

### Model Search & Final Architecture
**Architecture sweep:** tested **1,000+ model configurations** in TensorFlow/Keras across:
- Base architectures: Dense (FC), CNN, RNN, LSTM, BiLSTM, hybrids
- Depth: **4-layer** configurations
- Neurons per layer: **32, 64, 128, 256, 512** (all permutations)

Key findings:
- **128-neuron BiLSTM** outperformed larger (256/512) → larger models overfit.
- Pure CNNs missed temporal ordering; pure LSTMs lacked local feature extraction.
- **CNN-BiLSTM hybrid** outperformed pure architectures by **3–5% validation accuracy**.

**Final model (CNN-BiLSTM):**
- Input: **(150 timesteps, 6 channels)**
- CNN layers: local motion feature extraction across channels/time windows
- BiLSTM layers: bidirectional long-range temporal dependencies across 2.5s gesture
- Dense + Softmax: **62-class output**
- Regularization: **Dropout + L2**
- Optimizer: **Adam** (adaptive learning rate)
- Loss: **categorical cross-entropy**

### Evaluation Methodology
Two protocols:
1) **User-dependent (personalized):** per subject, **90% train (9 reps)** / **10% test (1 rep)**, repeated **10 times** with different splits.
2) **User-independent (generalization):** **leave-one-subject-out cross-validation** (train on 19, test on 1; repeat for all 20). Chosen to avoid data leakage from random splits.

### System Flow
- User writes character in air (2.5s)
- MPU6050 captures at 60Hz: **acc_x, acc_y, acc_z, gyro_x, gyro_y, gyro_z**
- ESP32 buffers **150 × 6**
- Wi‑Fi → Python backend
- Z-score normalization per channel
- Reshape to **(1, 150, 6)**
- CNN → BiLSTM → Softmax
- Output predicted character (A–Z, a–z, 0–9)

### Key Technical Decisions
- **2.5s @ 60Hz:** <2s truncates slow writers; >3s adds drift/noise + latency; 60Hz captures rapid direction changes with manageable size (**900 values/gesture**). 100Hz+ increased data volume ~4× with negligible accuracy gain in preliminary tests.
- **Wi‑Fi over Bluetooth:** removed Linux-only dependency (pyblue) and improved bandwidth/range.
- **Leave-one-subject-out CV:** most honest for generalization; quantified **18-point gap** between personalized vs cross-user.
- **IMU over computer vision:** works in any lighting, no line-of-sight, robust to occlusion/blur; integrates into wrist-wearable form.

### Results
- **User-dependent accuracy:** **75%**
- **User-independent accuracy:** **57%** (leave-one-subject-out)
- **User-dependent weighted precision/recall/F1:** **81% / 81% / 81%**
- **Per-class F1 range (user-dependent):** **59% – 100%**
- Confusions dominated by similar characters: **“O” vs “0”**, **“I” vs “l”**, **“S” vs “5”**

### Tech / Tags
Python, TensorFlow, Keras, CNN, BiLSTM, Deep Learning, ESP32, MPU6050, IMU Sensors, Gesture Recognition, Air Writing, Time Series Classification, Signal Processing, Z-Score Normalization, Hyperparameter Search, Cross-Validation, Wearable Computing, Wi‑Fi, I2C, NumPy, Embedded Systems, IIT Delhi

title: Project: AI-Powered Course Builder
content:
## Project: AI-Powered Course Builder (Automated Video Course Generation Platform for EHS Training)

**Overview:** Built an end-to-end AI-powered course generation system for an All-In-One EHS (Environment, Health & Safety) platform that converts a manual, week-long course creation process into an automated pipeline producing fully produced video courses in **under ~10 minutes**. The platform serves organizations across multiple industries and supports scalable training content.

### Problem
The client’s enterprise EHS platform already included course management features (course creator, **SCORM compliance**, responsive delivery, and **750+ prebuilt courses**). However, creating **custom courses** was highly manual and slow. Content teams spent **~1 week per course** doing repetitive work across multiple tools: selecting and cutting pre-recorded videos into smaller clips, merging segments, adding overlay text and transitions, layering background music, recording and syncing narration, etc. Growing demand made scaling impossible without scaling headcount.

### Solution (End-to-End Automation)
Designed and built an AI system that takes a **single input document** describing course content and autonomously executes the entire course-generation pipeline: intelligent video selection, clip assembly, narration generation, overlay text, transitions, background music selection, and visual-audio synchronization—producing a complete polished course in **~10 minutes**.

**What the AI automates:**
- **Intelligent Video Selection (RAG):** Searches **10,000+ pre-recorded video clips** and selects contextually relevant segments for each lesson.
- **Video Cutting & Assembly:** Automatically trims and merges clips into coherent lesson sequences.
- **AI Narration Generation:** Creates professional voiceover narration using **ElevenLabs**.
- **Overlay Text:** Generates and renders on-screen text reinforcing key points.
- **Transitions:** Adds smooth transitions between clips.
- **Background Music Selection:** Chooses background music from a curated library of **10–20 tracks**.
- **Visual-Audio Alignment:** A dedicated AI step reorders narration and clips so visuals are synchronized with audio.

### User Workflow (3-Phase Human-in-the-Loop)
- **Phase 1 — Course Outline Generation:** User uploads a course requirements document. AI generates a structured outline with lesson breakdowns, exam counts, and final exam configuration; user can review/edit/accept.
- **Phase 2 — Lesson Preview & Raw Output:** After approval, AI generates all lessons in parallel (RAG clip selection, narration, music, overlay text, transitions). Each lesson’s raw output is shown in a UI for preview/adjust/approve.
- **Phase 3 — Final Video Generation:** After confirmation, system renders the complete course (audio, transitions, overlays, music) via a **serverless processing pipeline**.

### Impact
- **Course creation time reduced from ~1 week to ~10 minutes** (~**99%** reduction).
- **Eliminated manual video editing** (no hand cutting/merging/syncing).
- **Scaled content production** on-demand **without additional headcount**.
- **Maintained quality control** through the 3-phase human-in-the-loop process.
- **Zero impact on existing infrastructure** by offloading video rendering to serverless.

### Tags
`Python` `FastAPI` `RAG` `Milvus` `Cohere` `Gemini 2.5 Pro` `ElevenLabs` `FFmpeg` `AWS Lambda` `AWS S3` `AWS SAM` `EC2` `Vector Search` `AI Video Generation` `SCORM` `EHS` `Course Automation`

---

## Technical Architecture: AI-Powered Course Builder (RAG + Video Processing + Serverless Rendering)

### Core Stack
| Component | Technology |
| --- | --- |
| Backend API | **Python**, **FastAPI** |
| Vector Database | **Milvus** |
| Embedding Model | **Cohere embed-english-v3.0** |
| Reranking Model | **Cohere rerank-v4.0-pro** |
| AI Model | **Google Gemini 2.5 Pro** |
| Voice Generation | **ElevenLabs API** |
| Video Processing | **FFmpeg** |
| Cloud Infrastructure | **AWS EC2**, **AWS Lambda**, **AWS S3**, **AWS SAM CLI** |

### Video Preprocessing Pipeline (Building an AI-Searchable Library)
Transforms **10,000+ raw videos** into a searchable, AI-ready clip library:
1. **Clip Segmentation:** Cut source videos into uniform **10-second clips** using **FFmpeg**.
2. **Visual Analysis:** Each clip analyzed with **Gemini 2.5 Pro** to generate detailed descriptions of visual content and movement.
3. **Embedding Generation:** Embed those descriptions using **Cohere embed-english-v3.0**.
4. **Vector Storage:** Index embeddings in **Milvus** with metadata for fast semantic retrieval.

### Lesson Generation Pipeline (Per Lesson, Parallelized)
1. **Document Partitioning:** Approved outline split into **N logical sections** (one per lesson).
2. **RAG Query Generation:** AI generates semantic queries from lesson content.
3. **Video Retrieval:** For a **~60-second** lesson clip, retrieve **~80–100 seconds** of candidate clips via RAG + **Cohere reranking**.
4. **AI Clip Selection & Narration (Single Inference):** **Gemini 2.5 Pro** reviews candidates, selects the best set, orders clips, and generates narration script in **one inference call**.
5. **Visual-Audio Alignment:** Secondary AI step where Gemini reorders and fine-tunes narration timing vs. clips to ensure sync.
6. **Production Elements:** AI selects music (from **10–20** tracks) and generates overlay text + transition specifications.

### Serverless Video Rendering (Preventing Server Overload)
- **AWS Lambda** executes compute-heavy video work (cutting, merging, overlay rendering, audio mixing, transitions) using **FFmpeg**.
- **AWS S3** stores video assets, intermediate files, and final outputs.
- **AWS SAM CLI** manages serverless deployment/config.
- Main **EC2** app server orchestrates the pipeline without taking the rendering load.

### Key Technical Decisions
- **Cohere embed-english-v3.0 + rerank-v4.0-pro:** Two-stage retrieval (embedding search → reranking) improves video selection quality; crucial when selecting **~60 seconds** of final footage from **~80–100 seconds** of candidates.
- **Gemini 2.5 Pro:** Chosen for multimodal video understanding and a large context window for narration writing, clip ordering, and alignment.
- **AWS Lambda for video processing:** Handles burst rendering compute elastically without affecting the client’s production server.
- **10-second segmentation:** Best granularity found—precise enough for RAG matching while preserving coherence and limiting vector count.

### Note
The source additionally states: **“everything happen in just 2 dollar”** (cost claim included as provided).

### Tags
`Python` `FastAPI` `RAG` `Milvus` `Cohere` `Gemini 2.5 Pro` `ElevenLabs` `FFmpeg` `AWS Lambda` `AWS S3` `AWS SAM` `EC2` `Vector Search` `AI Video Generation` `SCORM` `EHS` `Course Automation`

title: Project: AI-Powered Form Builder
content:
## Project: AI-Powered Form Builder (Enterprise EHS Platform) — PDF-to-Form Converter + Conversational AI Builder

**Automated form generation system** that converts **PDFs** and **natural language descriptions** into **fully structured digital forms** on a **multi-tenant EHS (Environmental Health & Safety) platform**. **Production system**; **200+ forms generated in the first month** via the PDF converter.

### Overview & Problem
The EHS platform already had a manual drag-and-drop form portal (input fields, checkboxes, radio buttons, date pickers, file uploads, conditional groups, etc.), but tenant onboarding required customers to submit **paper-based PDF templates** (safety inspection sheets, compliance checklists, incident report templates). The client’s team had to **recreate each form manually**.

- **Manual time per complex form:** **1+ hour** (nested containers, conditional logic, validation rules, complex structure)
- **Core complexity:** platform form schema is **deeply nested** (hierarchical groups/containers, legends, conditional checkboxes, conditional groups driven by radio options, arbitrary nesting)
- Result: major bottleneck consuming staff time and delaying tenant onboarding.

### Solution (Two-Part System)
Built a two-part AI solution after examining the existing form system and understanding the platform’s complex JSON schema:

1) **PDF-to-Form Converter:** Upload PDF(s) → generates a complete digital form replica in the platform’s native format.
   - Supports **bulk upload** of **100+ PDFs**; processes in **5–10 minutes**.

2) **Conversational AI Form Builder:** Guided interface for creating new forms from a natural language description.
   - Produces a human-reviewable **markdown blueprint** that users can iterate before final generation.

Both tools output forms that are **immediately usable** in the platform with **no manual adjustment required**.

### Key Architecture: Intermediate JSON Strategy
The platform’s **native form JSON** was too structurally complex for reliable one-pass LLM generation (wrapper objects, aliasing conventions, type-specific properties, nesting). To make generation robust:

- Designed a **simplified intermediate JSON schema** validated by **Pydantic**.
- Pipeline: **LLM → intermediate JSON → deterministic conversion → native platform JSON**.
- Benefit: decouples AI understanding (form intent/structure) from deterministic correctness (native JSON), enabling **perfect structural accuracy**.

**Intermediate schema supports:**
- **Simple fields:** Input, Email, Number, Phone Number, Signature Box
- **Option fields:** Checkbox, Radio Button, Select Box (configurable values)
- **User dropdowns:** dynamic user lists filtered by role (**users, managers, all-users, users-and-managers**)
- **Rich text editors:** Textarea with optional HTML formatting
- **Date/Time fields:** date picker, time picker, range selection, past/future restrictions
- **File upload fields:** per-type toggles (**audio, video, image, document**), single/multi upload
- **Containers:** Groups (card-style), Legends (border-style), Conditional Checkboxes (show-on-check), Conditional Groups (radio-driven sections)
- **Arbitrary nesting:** containers within containers to any depth

### Part 1: PDF-to-Form Converter (Flow + Performance)
**Flow:**
- User uploads PDF(s) → PDF content extracted (text, structure, layout analysis) → **GPT-4o** analyzes PDF and generates **intermediate JSON** → **Pydantic validation** → programmatic conversion to **native form JSON** → form available in platform.

**Performance:**
- **Single form generation:** ~**7,000 tokens**, ~**20 seconds** per form
- **Bulk upload:** **100+ PDFs** in parallel → **5–10 minutes** total
- Replaces **100+ hours** of manual work for 100 forms.

**Model:** **GPT-4o** (document understanding + structured output) for PDF analysis and intermediate JSON generation.

### Part 2: Conversational AI Form Builder (Multi-Step, Multi-Model)
Goal: enable non-technical tenants to create new forms from scratch without knowing the form builder UI.

**Conversational flow:**
1. User provides brief description (e.g., “workplace safety inspection for construction sites”).
2. **GPT-4o** generates: suggested form title, form type classification, categories, and clarifying questions (checkbox/radio). User reviews/answers.
3. **Qwen3-Coder-480B** (via Together AI) generates a **markdown blueprint** showing sections, fields, containers, conditional logic.
4. User reviews blueprint; provides refinement feedback if needed; **Qwen3-Coder** regenerates until approved.
5. **GPT-4o-mini** converts approved blueprint → **intermediate JSON → native form JSON**.

**Multi-model rationale (production):**
- **GPT-4o:** best at interpreting vague intent into structured metadata.
- **Qwen3-Coder-480B-A35B-Instruct-FP8 (Together AI):** tested vs GPT-4o; produced **better-structured blueprints**, more accurate field groupings, and conditional logic; runs via Together AI inference API.
- **GPT-4o-mini:** reliable for constrained conversion of an approved blueprint to intermediate JSON; lower cost and faster.

### Stack
- **Backend:** Python, FastAPI
- **Models:** GPT-4o (PDF analysis, metadata), Qwen3-Coder-480B-A35B-Instruct-FP8 via Together AI (blueprint generation/refinement), GPT-4o-mini (final JSON generation)
- **Validation:** Pydantic schema enforcement on intermediate JSON
- **Infrastructure:** production deployment on the client’s existing EHS platform

### Key Technical Decisions
- **Intermediate JSON vs native JSON generation:** native JSON’s wrappers/aliases/type-specific fields are error-prone for LLMs; intermediate schema is LLM-friendly; **Pydantic** catches structural issues; deterministic conversion ensures valid native output.
- **Three models:** different tasks (intent/metadata vs blueprint structuring vs constrained JSON conversion) optimized for quality, cost, latency.
- **Blueprint-first iterative refinement:** prevents committing errors in complex, nested, conditional forms; improves user satisfaction and reduces post-generation edits.
- **Bulk upload:** onboarding requires entire form libraries; batch + parallel processing converts days of work into a 5–10 minute operation.
- **Pydantic validation:** catches invalid JSON/missing required fields/wrong types; enables re-prompting/flagging before producing broken forms.

### Impact (Measured)
- **Time per form (from PDF):** 1+ hour manual → **~20 seconds**
- **Bulk migration (100 forms):** 100+ staff-hours → **5–10 minutes**
- **Forms generated (PDF converter, month 1):** **200+**
- **Forms generated (AI builder, month 1):** **15+**
- **Manual drag-and-drop required:** every field → **none**
- **Tenant self-service form creation:** not possible → **available via AI builder**

Estimated **99%+ reduction in form creation time** from the PDF converter; conversational builder extended creation directly to tenants.

**Tags:** FastAPI, Python, GPT-4o, GPT-4o-mini, Qwen3-Coder-480B, Together AI, Pydantic, PDF Processing, Form Generation, Multi-Model Architecture, Intermediate Schema, EHS Platform, Multi-Tenant, Production System, Bulk Processing

title: Project: Multi-Tenancy RAG Chatbot
content:
## Project: Multi-Tenancy RAG Chatbot — Permission-Aware AI Assistant for an Enterprise EHS Platform

Built a **multi-tenant RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) chatbot** for an enterprise **EHS platform** that serves **hundreds of organizations**, each with unique user roles, permissions, and course assignments. The system delivers **accurate, context-aware answers in ~8 seconds** while enforcing **250+ granular permissions per user in real time**—ensuring users never see restricted information. Achieved **80% MRR (Mean Reciprocal Rank)** across a complex **multi-source knowledge base** serving **~1,000+ queries/day**.

### Problem & Constraints (Multi-Tenant + RBAC)
- **250+ distinct permissions** assignable in any combination → thousands of permission profiles.
- **750+ courses**, individually assignable → users must only get answers for courses they can access.
- **Page-aware context** → chatbot must tailor responses based on the page the user is viewing.
- **Multi-source knowledge** → answers must combine website content, knowledge base, manually authored content, and course materials.
- **Conversation continuity** → multi-turn dialog while enforcing permissions every step.
- **Multilingual support** (e.g., French).
- **No viable caching**: users can have different answers for the same query due to unique permissions, and permissions change frequently.

### Solution Architecture (2-Stage RAG + Agentic Orchestration)
Designed a **2-stage RAG system** with a **dedicated permission-filtering layer before retrieval**, enforcing access control at the **query level** rather than statically partitioning the index. This decouples permission logic from the knowledge base and avoids re-indexing when permissions change. The pipeline is orchestrated end-to-end as a **LangGraph** state machine — permission filtering, retrieval, tool-calling (platform APIs), and answer generation are explicit graph nodes — built on top of **LangChain** agents and tool abstractions, giving the chatbot the ability to autonomously fetch live data and trigger workflows in addition to answering knowledge-base questions.

### How It Works
#### Stage 1 — Permission-Aware Query Preprocessing
1. **Permission similarity search**: embed user query and search a dedicated **Milvus permission index**; retrieve **top 30** semantically similar permissions.
2. **Cross-reference**: match those candidates against the user’s actual permission set stored in **PostgreSQL**, producing lists of permissions the user **has** vs **lacks**.
3. **AI-powered query filtering (GPT-4o)**: send matched/unmatched permissions + **last 5 conversation turns**; GPT-4o performs one of:
   - **Full access**: rewrites into a clear, self-contained query (incorporating conversation context) → passes to Stage 2.
   - **No access**: generates a denial response (e.g., “You don’t have permission to edit users.”) → **terminate early** (no retrieval).
   - **Partial access (compound queries)**: splits the query; permitted part is rewritten for Stage 2 and a denial for restricted part is appended to the final response.
4. **Conversation history resolution**: resolves coreference across turns (e.g., “edit one” → “edit a user”) to keep retrieval queries standalone while preserving natural multi-turn flow.

#### Stage 2 — Multi-Source Retrieval & Answer Generation
- Search a second **Milvus content index** containing **10,000+ chunks** across all sources.
- **Data sources**:
  - Website pages (crawled documentation/help)
  - Knowledge base articles
  - External chunks (explicit supplementary content)
  - Course content (**750+ courses**) stored with **course IDs as metadata** (~**100 chunks dedicated to course content**)

**Course-level filtering**: for course-related questions, apply **Milvus metadata filtering** to include only courses the user is assigned to (course IDs fetched from PostgreSQL). This is a **hard vector-search filter**: unauthorized course chunks are never retrieved or shown to the LLM.

**Page-aware context**: frontend sends the **current page URL** with each request; system maps URL to its chunk and **injects that page chunk** into LLM context alongside the **top 5 retrieved chunks**, enabling page-specific answers.

**Answer generation**: GPT-4o receives rewritten query + last 5 turns + (top 5 RAG chunks + current page chunk) and produces an **HTML response** with formatting and **source citations (direct links)**. End-to-end latency is **~8 seconds**, using **8,000–15,000 tokens/request**.

### Impact
- **~8 seconds** end-to-end response time with real-time permission enforcement
- **80% MRR** on complex multi-source knowledge base
- **250+ permissions enforced per query** (no re-indexing, no static partitioning)
- **750+ courses dynamically filtered** per user at vector-search time
- **500+ concurrent users supported** with **4 FastAPI workers**
- **~100 daily active users**, **~1,000 queries/day**
- Compound query handling (split permitted + denied actions)
- Multilingual support via translation bridge; extensible to **100+ languages** without RAG pipeline changes
- Page-aware responses with source transparency (direct links)
- Deployed on client’s existing **AWS EC2** server (zero additional infrastructure)

### Tags
`Python` `FastAPI` `LangChain` `LangGraph` `Agentic AI` `RAG` `Milvus` `GPT-4o` `PostgreSQL` `Multi-Tenancy` `RBAC` `Permission Filtering` `Tool Calling` `HTML Responses` `Conversation Management` `EHS Platform` `AWS EC2`

title: Project: Multi-Tenancy RAG Chatbot
content:
## Project: Multi-Tenancy RAG Chatbot — Technical Architecture, Ingestion, and Key Decisions

This project is a **permission-aware, multi-tenant RAG chatbot** for an enterprise **EHS platform**, designed to answer questions in **~8 seconds** while enforcing **250+ granular permissions** per user and retrieving from a **10,000+ chunk** multi-source knowledge base.

### Core Technical Stack
| Component | Technology |
| --- | --- |
| Backend API | **Python, FastAPI (4 workers)** |
| Agent Framework | **LangChain (agents + tool calling over 10+ platform APIs)** |
| Agent Orchestration | **LangGraph (stateful graph: permission filter → retrieval → tools → answer)** |
| Vector Database | **Milvus (2 indexes)** |
| Embedding Models | **OpenAI text-embedding-3-large**, **BGE-M3 (16-bit precision)** |
| LLM | **GPT-4o** |
| Relational Database | **PostgreSQL** |
| Web Crawler | **Selenium (custom-built)** |
| Translation | **Google Translate API** |
| Deployment | **AWS EC2** (integrated into client’s existing server) |
| Response Format | **HTML with source citations** |

### Performance Metrics
| Metric | Value |
| --- | --- |
| Response Time | **~8 seconds end-to-end** |
| Token Usage | **8,000–15,000 tokens/request** |
| Concurrent Capacity | **500+ simultaneous users** |
| Daily Active Users | **~100 users** |
| Daily Query Volume | **~1,000 queries/day** |
| MRR Score | **80%** |
| Total Chunks Indexed | **10,000+** |

### Milvus Index Design (2 Indexes)
| Index | Purpose | Contents |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Permission Index** | Query-level access control | **250+ permission descriptions** embedded as vectors |
| **Content Index** | Answer generation | **10,000+ chunks** from website pages, knowledge base, external content, and **750+ courses** (with **course ID metadata**) |

### Embedding Strategy
- **OpenAI text-embedding-3-large**: used for the **content index** where retrieval precision most impacts answer quality.
- **BGE-M3 at 16-bit precision**: chosen for **GPU memory efficiency** (reduced VRAM usage without meaningful accuracy loss), enabling operation on cost-effective infrastructure.

### Smart / Adaptive Chunking Strategy
- **Short pages (≤500 tokens)**: stored as **single chunks** (avoid context fragmentation).
- **Long pages (>500 tokens)**: split into **300-token chunks** with **100-token overlap** (captures boundary-spanning info).
- This practical balance contributed to the project’s **80% MRR** by minimizing fragmentation while preserving retrieval precision.

### Data Ingestion Pipeline
**Website crawling (Selenium)**
- Custom crawler that handles dynamic UI content, not just static scraping.
- Recognizes interactive elements tagged with CSS classes like **`aiclickableclass`** and programmatically clicks through to capture hidden content behind **tabs, dropdowns, accordions, expandable sections**.

**Content processing**
- All sources are converted to **Markdown** before chunking and embedding.
- Markdown preserves structure (headings, tables, lists), improving LLM understanding and answer quality.

**Course ingestion**
- Each course is chunked and stored in Milvus with **course ID metadata**.
- At query time: system queries **PostgreSQL** for user’s assigned course IDs and applies them as **inclusion filters** in Milvus to ensure only accessible courses are retrieved.

### Multilingual Support (Translation Bridge)
1. UI provides a language selector.
2. Non-English query (e.g., French) is translated to English via **Google Translate** before entering the RAG pipeline.
3. Retrieval + generation runs in English (leveraging English-optimized embeddings + GPT-4o).
4. Final English response is translated back to the selected language.
- Adding support for new languages requires **zero changes** to embeddings, index, or RAG pipeline; only the translation layer is used (Google Translate supports **100+ languages**).

### Conversation Management
- Conversations persisted in **PostgreSQL** with full turn history.
- On each message, the **last 5 turns** are loaded and passed through the permission-filtering AI step, combining permission logic + context resolution with **one inference call**.
- 5-turn window balances conversational coherence with the **8k–15k token** budget.

### System Flow (High-Level)
- User sends message + current page URL + language
- If non-English: translate to English
- **Stage 1**: permission filtering (embed → search permission index top 30 → cross-ref PostgreSQL perms → GPT-4o decides full/none/partial)
- **Stage 2**: content retrieval & generation (search content index with course filters → retrieve top 5 chunks → inject current page chunk → GPT-4o generates **HTML** with **source links**)
- If non-English: translate response back
- Total: **~8 seconds**, **8k–15k tokens**

### Key Technical Decisions (Rationale)
- **2 Milvus indexes vs 1**: permissions are volatile; keeping permissions separate avoids re-indexing or large-scale metadata updates across content.
- **AI-based permission filtering vs rules**: with **250+ permissions**, rule mapping is brittle; semantic search + LLM handles nuance (e.g., compound queries, implicit requirements).
- **No caching**: unique permission profiles + frequent changes → negligible hit rate; added complexity without meaningful performance gain.
- **BGE-M3 16-bit**: reduced GPU memory requirements vs 32-bit; negligible accuracy loss.
- **Adaptive chunking**: preserves short pages intact; splits only when necessary (300/100 overlap) to protect retrieval quality.
- **Translate-bridge vs multilingual embeddings**: knowledge base is English; English-only models perform better on English content; translation overhead is minor relative to LLM time.
- **HTML responses**: enables rich formatting (steps, lists, tables, links) and embeds source citations directly for usability and trust.

### Tags
`LangChain` `LangGraph` `Agentic AI` `Tool Calling` `Milvus` `OpenAI text-embedding-3-large` `BGE-M3` `Selenium` `Google Translate` `Markdown Processing` `Web Crawling` `Permission Filtering` `RBAC` `FastAPI` `PostgreSQL` `AWS EC2` `GPT-4o`

title: Project: Voicer
content:
## Project: Voicer — Sign Language to Speech (Wearable TinyML Device)

**Voicer** is a wearable AI device that translates **Indian Sign Language (ISL)** gestures into **spoken audio in real time** using **on-device machine learning** (no internet required). Recognitions: **Samsung Solve for Tomorrow — Top 10 finalist among 70,000+ participants**; **featured on CNN News18**.

### Overview
- Wearable system with **10 IMU sensors (one per finger)** capturing hand movement at **60Hz**.
- Runs a **custom neural network entirely on a NodeMCU microcontroller**.
- Sends recognized gesture label to a **mobile app via Bluetooth**, which plays the corresponding audio.
- **On-device inference cycle: 2 ms**.
- Model performance (12 gesture classes): **94.1% training accuracy**, **92.8% test accuracy**.
- Edge deployment footprint: **55.1KB flash**, **2.0KB RAM**.

### Problem
- ~**72 million** people worldwide rely on sign language; **10 million in India**.
- India has ~**300 certified sign language interpreters** (~**1 per 33,000** deaf/hard-of-hearing individuals).
- Interpreters are expensive/inconvenient and not available in everyday contexts.
- Existing tech often targets **ASL/BSL**, is **camera/phone dependent**, and lacks portability.

### Solution
- User wears **10 sensor rings** plus a **wrist controller unit**.
- Sensors capture motion → **on-device ML classifies** gesture → label transmitted by **Bluetooth** → mobile app plays **audio output**.
- Built for real-world usage: **no phone camera**, **no cloud**, **no connectivity dependency**, low latency.
- Team visited **sign language classes** and learned **ISL** to ensure dataset/gestures match authentic use.

### Team & Role (Team Hackvengers — 3 members)
- **Amit Gohel (my contributions):**
  - Designed/trained the **TinyML gesture classification model** on **Edge Impulse**, testing multiple architectures to reach **94.1% accuracy** within microcontroller constraints.
  - Built the **custom dataset**: coordinated collection from **10 people**, **20 repetitions per gesture**, yielding **200 samples/class** across **12 classes**.
  - **Hardware assembly/integration**: wired **10 IMU sensors** to **NodeMCU** and integrated with the **3D-printed wrist unit**.
  - Implemented **preprocessing**: **spectral feature extraction** + **Z-score normalization** for cross-user consistency.
  - **Optimized for edge**: compressed model to **55.1KB flash** and **2.0KB RAM**.
  - Learned ISL via **sign language classes** to ensure gesture accuracy.
- **Varun Thummar:** Bluetooth communication + real-time transmission code between device and mobile app.
- **Aditi Patel:** research, presentation preparation, and competition pitch.

### Hardware cost (MVP)
- Controller (NodeMCU): **₹2,000**
- Custom PCB: **₹2,000**
- IMU sensors (10): **₹2,800**
- Wires & connectors: **₹500**
- Audio module: **₹2,500**
- 3D-printed enclosure (design + print): **₹1,000**
- Server charges: **₹1,000**
- Maintenance: **₹900**
- **Total:** ~**₹13,000 (~$155)**

### Media & Links
- GitHub: https://github.com/amit-gohel/voicer
- Samsung feature: https://bit.ly/3Lpzwp7
- CNN News18 episode: https://youtu.be/mYB_ZDl1Ngc?si=EOKG5D37gGG-YLRy
- Samsung India (pre-competition video): https://x.com/SamsungNewsIN/status/1697949999186010136?s=20
- Journey video & final pitch (Facebook): https://www.facebook.com/cnnnews18/videos/1011721290157892/
- Device photos/videos: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1tpLVUuKZlnIZsIs_dJ6AgB7Q-GawhA8g?usp=drive_link

### Tags
`TinyML` `Edge AI` `Edge Impulse` `NodeMCU` `ESP32` `IMU Sensors` `Accelerometer` `Gyroscope` `Gesture Recognition` `Sign Language` `Indian Sign Language` `TensorFlow Lite` `Neural Network` `Quantization` `INT8` `Spectral Features` `Z-Score Normalization` `Bluetooth` `3D Printing` `Wearable Device` `Samsung Solve for Tomorrow` `CNN News18` `Social Impact`

title: Project: Voicer
content:
## Project: Voicer — Technical Architecture, Dataset, Model, and Results

**Voicer** is a wearable **ISL (Indian Sign Language) gesture-to-speech** translator using **10 IMU sensors** (one per finger), **NodeMCU** on-device inference, and a **Bluetooth mobile app** for audio playback. Key claim: **real-time inference in 2 ms**, offline.

### Hardware design
- **Finger rings (10 units):** each contains an **IMU** with **accelerometer + gyroscope**.
  - Per IMU reading: **6 values** (Accel X/Y/Z + Gyro X/Y/Z).
  - Across 10 fingers: **60 raw values per time step**.
- **Wrist controller:** **3D-printed enclosure** housing **NodeMCU microcontroller**; routes all 10 sensors; runs ML model locally; handles Bluetooth.
- **Mobile app:** receives classified label via **Bluetooth** and plays corresponding **audio**.

### Sensor sampling & feature generation
- Sampling rate: **60Hz**.
- Gesture window: **2 seconds** → **120 time steps**.
- Raw data per sample: **120 × 60 = 7,200 raw data points**.
- Edge Impulse preprocessing:
  - **Spectral feature extraction** compresses time-series into **234 frequency-domain features**.
  - **Z-score normalization** across sensor channels to improve generalization across users (hand size, strength, speed differences).

### Dataset & data collection
- Dataset built specifically for **Indian Sign Language**; team learned ISL via **sign language classes**.
- **12 gesture classes:** Afternoon, Clapping, Crying, Everyone, Family, Good, Hello, India, Noice, Playing, Sorry, Thank You.
- Collection protocol:
  - **10 participants** × **20 repetitions** per gesture → **200 samples/class**.
  - Each sample: **2 seconds** at **60Hz**.
  - Total training data: **17 minutes 22 seconds**.
  - **845 training windows** extracted.
- Earlier prototype dataset:
  - **6 classes**, **15 minutes**, **775 training windows**.
  - Gestures: Hello, Thank You, Solve, For Tomorrow, Judges, Noice.

### Model architecture (Edge Impulse, quantized)
Neural network designed for microcontroller constraints:
```
Input Layer  → 234 features
Dense Layer  → 128 neurons
Dense Layer  → 64 neurons
Dense Layer  → 32 neurons
Dense Layer  → 16 neurons
Output Layer → 12 classes (softmax)
```
- Rationale: progressive bottleneck **128→64→32→16** reduces parameters while keeping discriminative power.
- **Quantization:** **INT8** for deployment, reducing memory footprint.

### On-device performance
| Metric | 6-Class Model | 12-Class Model |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Training Accuracy | 94.8% | 94.1% |
| Training Loss | 0.14 | 0.40 |
| Test Accuracy | — | 92.8% |
| Inference Time | 60 ms | 2 ms |
| Peak RAM Usage | 2.6 KB | 2.0 KB |
| Flash Usage | 228.0 KB | 55.1 KB |

- 12-class competition model: **2 ms inference**, **2.0KB RAM**, **55.1KB flash** (~500 classifications/sec).

### Per-class performance (12-class model — test set)
| Gesture | Accuracy | F1 Score |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Afternoon | 100% | 0.95 |
| Clapping | 94.7% | 0.97 |
| Crying | 100% | 1.00 |
| Everyone | 90% | 0.95 |
| Family | 87.5% | 0.88 |
| Good | 93.8% | 0.94 |
| Hello | 100% | 1.00 |
| India | 66.7% | 0.73 |
| Noice | 88.2% | 0.94 |
| Playing | 100% | 1.00 |
| Sorry | 85.7% | 0.92 |
| Thank You | 100% | 0.94 |

- Most gestures: **90%+ accuracy**; **“India”** lowest at **66.7%** (subtle finger movements overlapping with gestures like “Hello” and “Noice” in sensor space).

### System flow
```
User performs sign language gesture
        │
        ▼
10 IMU sensors capture accel+gyro at 60Hz
(60 values × 120 time steps = 7,200 raw data points)
        │
        ▼
Spectral feature extraction + Z-score normalization → 234 features
        │
        ▼
On-device neural network inference (NodeMCU, 2ms)
        │
        ▼
Gesture label sent via Bluetooth to mobile app
        │
        ▼
Mobile app plays audio output
```

### Key technical decisions (as implemented)
- **IMU vs camera:** wearable works in any direction/lighting; no need to face a camera.
- **10 IMUs (one per finger):** captures fine finger articulation; wrist-only IMU would miss key distinctions.
- **Spectral features:** compresses **7,200 raw points → 234 features** (~30×) enabling microcontroller-sized model.
- **On-device inference:** avoids internet dependency; low latency and privacy benefits.
- **Edge Impulse:** end-to-end pipeline (collection→preprocessing→training→INT8 quantization→firmware export) suitable for tight **flash/RAM** constraints.

title: Project: WeepScope
content:
## Project: WeepScope — Infant Cry Classifier (Standalone TinyML Device)

**WeepScope** is a standalone hardware device that listens to a crying baby and identifies the reason behind the distress **in real time**, classifying cries into **five Dunstan Baby Language categories**: **hunger, sleepiness, discomfort, lower gas, and need to burp** (plus a **noise** class in the dataset/model). The system runs a custom-trained **hybrid 1D CNN + GRU** model **entirely on-device** on an **Arduino Nano 33 BLE Sense (1MB flash, 256KB SRAM)** and displays the predicted class on an integrated **0.96-inch OLED**—**no phone, no internet, no app** required.

**Problem context:** WHO estimates ~**139 million births/year**, and roughly **20% (~27.8 million couples)** are first-time parents who often struggle to interpret crying. Dunstan Baby Language suggests characteristic cry patterns: **"Neh" (hunger), "Heh" (discomfort), "Owh" (sleepiness), "Eairh" (lower gas), "Eh" (burp)**, but these are difficult for untrained parents to distinguish. While **fewer than 10 mobile apps** attempt this, WeepScope targets the gap: **dedicated passive hardware** that classifies and displays results without phone interaction.

**Device behavior / reliability:** WeepScope continuously listens via the built-in microphone; upon detecting crying it:
1) captures a **2-second** audio sample at **16 kHz**,
2) extracts features,
3) runs on-device inference,
4) repeats **5 consecutive** classifications, and
5) applies **majority voting** to reduce false identifications in real-world noise, then
6) displays the final output on the OLED.

**Topline results:** Achieved **88.7% validation accuracy** and **86.03% test accuracy** on a custom dataset of **>2 hours** of classified infant cry audio (main model). Compressed on-device model achieved **78.23% test accuracy**.

**Link:** GitHub repository: https://github.com/amit-gohel/Weep-Scope

**Tags:** TinyML, Edge AI, Edge Impulse, Arduino Nano 33 BLE Sense, Sound Classification, 1D CNN, GRU, Mel-Filterbank Energy, Audio Processing, Infant Cry Analysis, Dunstan Baby Language, INT8 Quantization, 3D Printing, OLED Display, Embedded Systems, Research Paper, IoT, Healthcare

---

## Project: WeepScope — Technical Architecture, Dataset, Models, Hardware, Research

### Dataset creation (built from scratch)
No clean pre-labeled dataset existed for the five Dunstan cry categories, so the WeepScope dataset was curated and labeled manually.

- **Source:** Multiple YouTube playlists of crying babies in various contexts.
- **Labeling process:** Each audio file was manually compared against Dunstan Baby Language reference samples and labeled as one of **five cry types** or **noise**.

**Dataset summary (2-second samples):**

| Class | Total Duration | Total Samples (2-sec) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Hungry | 17m 2s | 511 |
| Discomfort | 17m 52s | 536 |
| Sleepy | 18m 22s | 551 |
| Lower Gas | 11m 16s | 338 |
| Burp | 26m 16s | 788 |
| Noise | 29m 42s | 891 |
| **Total** | **2h 0m 25s** | **3,615** |

- **Segmentation strategy:** Tested **1s, 2s, 3s, 5s** windows; **2 seconds** gave highest accuracy. Used overlapping sliding windows (e.g., a 10s clip yields 0–2s, 1–3s, 2–4s, …) to preserve pattern integrity and expand training data.
- **Train/test split:** **1h 36m 3s** training, **24m 23s** testing.

### Audio preprocessing & features
- **Sampling rate:** **16 kHz**.
- **Feature extraction:** Compared **MFE (Mel-Filterbank Energy)**, **MFCC**, and **Spectrogram**; **MFE** produced the highest accuracy.
- **MFE parameters tuned experimentally:** Frame Length, Frame Stride, Filter Number, FFT Length, Low/High Frequency cutoffs, Noise Floor (dB).
- **Feature output size:** Each 2-second sample becomes **3,100 features**.

### Model architectures
Two models were developed: a full-accuracy model and a compressed deployable model for Arduino memory constraints.

**Main model (1D CNN + GRU hybrid):**
```
Input Layer     → 3,100 features
Reshape Layer   → 50 columns
1D Conv Layer   → 64 neurons
Dropout         → 0.2 rate
1D Conv Layer   → 128 neurons
Dropout         → 0.2 rate
1D Conv Layer   → 256 neurons
Dropout         → 0.2 rate
GRU Layer       → 64 neurons
Flatten Layer
Output Layer    → 6 classes (softmax)
```
- Rationale: CNN layers learn local spectral patterns; GRU captures temporal evolution over the 2-second window; **dropout 0.2** reduces overfitting on a relatively small dataset.

**Compressed model (1D CNN only):**
- GRU removed to reduce model size to fit **Arduino Nano 33 BLE Sense (1MB flash, 256KB SRAM)**.

### Performance comparison

| Metric | Main Model | Compressed Model |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Architecture | 1D CNN + GRU | 1D CNN only |
| Validation Accuracy | 88.7% | 82.6% |
| Test Accuracy | 86.03% | 78.23% |
| Loss | 0.37 | — |
| Inference Latency | 4,252 ms | 1,220 ms |
| Flash Usage | 882.0 KB | 168.3 KB |

- The compressed model was deployed on Arduino and paired with **5-cycle majority voting**, making **78.23%** test accuracy acceptable in practice.

### Per-class test performance (Main model)

| Cry Type | F1 Score |
| --- | --- |
| Burp | 0.89 |
| Discomfort | 0.84 |
| Hungry | 0.92 |
| Lower Gas | 0.76 |
| Noise | 0.99 |
| Sleepy | 0.81 |

- Most reliable: **Noise (0.99 F1)** and **Hungry (0.92 F1)**.
- Most challenging: **Lower Gas (0.76 F1)**.

### Hardware implementation
**Components:**

| Component | Details |
| --- | --- |
| Microcontroller | Arduino Nano 33 BLE Sense |
| Memory | 1MB flash, 256KB SRAM |
| Microphone | Built-in PDM microphone (on-board) |
| Display | 0.96-inch OLED display |
| Power | Battery-powered |
| Enclosure | Custom 3D-printed case designed to attach to baby cradle |

**On-device inference pipeline:** capture 2s audio @ 16kHz → MFE extraction (**3,100 features**) → compressed 1D CNN inference (~**1.2 sec**) → repeat 5 times → majority vote → display result.

### System flow
```
Baby starts crying
        │
        ▼
Arduino built-in microphone captures
2 seconds of audio at 16kHz
        │
        ▼
Mel-Filterbank Energy (MFE) extraction
→ 3,100 acoustic features
        │
        ▼
On-device 1D CNN inference
(Arduino Nano 33 BLE Sense, ~1.2 sec)
        │
        ▼
Repeat 5 times → Majority voting
        │
        ▼
Display result on OLED screen:
Hungry | Sleepy | Discomfort |
Lower Gas | Burp | Noise
```

### Key technical decisions captured in the write-up
- **MFE vs MFCC/Spectrogram:** MFE best accuracy; MFCC’s DCT step discards some energy info; raw spectrograms too large for efficient on-device processing.
- **Hybrid 1D CNN + GRU:** CNN captures frequency patterns; GRU captures temporal dynamics.
- **2-second windows:** Best balance vs 1s (insufficient pattern) and 3–5s (more noise + more latency).
- **5-cycle majority voting:** Filters transient noise/transition states at cost of ~6 seconds total processing.
- **Standalone device vs mobile app:** “Place-and-forget” cradle-mounted device with glanceable OLED output.
- **Model compression vs more powerful board:** Arduino chosen for small form factor, low power, built-in mic; compressing model preferred over larger/more power-hungry boards.

### Research context
WeepScope was developed as a research collaboration with **Dr. Kirankumar Trivedi (Northeastern University, USA)** and produced a written **research paper** documenting methodology, architecture, dataset creation, and experimental results. The work builds on prior infant cry analysis and Dunstan Baby Language, contributing a novel hardware implementation using TinyML techniques. Inspiration/extension noted: Dr. Trivedi’s embedded sound classification work, including a mosquito identification system using wing-beat sound classification on tiny embedded systems (published at **ITU Kaleidoscope 2021**).

title: Project: SAP Analytics Bot
content:
## Project: SAP Analytics Bot — Natural Language to SQL with Auto-Dashboard Generation

**SAP Analytics Bot** is a production Text-to-SQL system built at **Silver Touch Technologies** that lets business users ask questions in plain English against a live SAP-connected relational database and receive answers, statistical summaries, and **auto-generated dashboard charts** — no SQL knowledge required. The system goes beyond query generation: it runs the SQL, intelligently preprocesses large result sets, and instructs an LLM to generate **2 contextual graphs** rendered directly in the dashboard alongside the answer.

**Problem context:** Business analysts at the client organization were dependent on SAP developers to pull reports — a process that took hours to days. The database had **100+ tables** with SAP-style cryptic column names and complex foreign-key relationships. The goal was a conversational bot where analysts type a question and get a complete answer with visuals in under 30 seconds.

**Topline results:** End-to-end query success rate of **~89%** on business analyst test queries. Self-refinement step resolved ambiguous table selection in **~70% of multi-table queries**. Average response time: **~18 seconds** including SQL execution and chart generation.

**Tech Stack:** Python, FastAPI, Qdrant, BGE-M3 embeddings, LLaMA 3.1 (via VLLM), PostgreSQL, SQLAlchemy, Pandas.

**Tags:** Text-to-SQL, NL2SQL, SAP, RAG, Qdrant, Schema Retrieval, BGE-M3, LLaMA, VLLM, Auto-Dashboard, Chart Generation, FastAPI, PostgreSQL, Self-Refinement, Silver Touch Technologies

---

## Project: SAP Analytics Bot — Full Pipeline, Schema Indexing, Self-Refinement & Dashboard Generation

**SAP Analytics Bot** is a **5-stage Text-to-SQL pipeline** — schema retrieval via **Qdrant**, LLM-driven table self-refinement, SQL generation, intelligent result preprocessing, and **auto chart generation** — built to give SAP business users instant answers with visual dashboards from plain English questions.

### Stage 1: Offline Schema Indexing (Table Chunk Preparation)

The core insight: an LLM writes better SQL when it understands not just column names but what the data *actually looks like*. Each table was converted into a rich schema chunk containing two things:

**1. DDL (Data Definition Language):** Full CREATE TABLE statement including column names, data types, constraints, and foreign keys.

**2. 5 Random Sample Rows:** Real rows sampled from the table, showing actual value formats, enum values, and naming conventions the LLM would need to generate correct WHERE clauses.

Example chunk for one table:
```
Table: VBAK (Sales Order Header)
DDL:
  CREATE TABLE VBAK (
    VBELN VARCHAR(10) PRIMARY KEY,  -- Sales order number
    ERDAT DATE,                      -- Creation date
    AUART VARCHAR(4),                -- Order type: 'TA'=Standard, 'KR'=Returns
    KUNNR VARCHAR(10),               -- Customer number (FK → KNA1)
    NETWR NUMERIC(15,2),             -- Net order value
    WAERK VARCHAR(5)                 -- Currency: 'INR', 'USD'
  );

Sample rows:
| VBELN      | ERDAT      | AUART | KUNNR      | NETWR      | WAERK |
|------------|------------|-------|------------|------------|-------|
| 0000012345 | 2024-03-15 | TA    | 0000087234 | 142500.00  | INR   |
| 0000012346 | 2024-03-15 | KR    | 0000091100 | -8200.00   | INR   |
| 0000012347 | 2024-03-16 | TA    | 0000087234 | 67800.50   | USD   |
| 0000012348 | 2024-03-17 | TA    | 0000055678 | 310000.00  | INR   |
| 0000012349 | 2024-03-17 | TA    | 0000087234 | 22100.00   | INR   |
```
- These 5 rows gave the LLM critical context: that `AUART = 'TA'` means a standard order, that `NETWR` is negative for returns, and that `KUNNR` is zero-padded.
- Each chunk was embedded using **BGE-M3** and stored in **Qdrant** — one vector per table.
- **Total chunks indexed:** ~110 table chunks covering the full SAP schema.

### Stage 2: Query Expansion — LLM Generates Search Queries

User questions are often vague or use business terminology that doesn't map directly to SAP column names. Instead of embedding the raw user question for retrieval, an LLM first **rewrites it into 3–5 semantically rich search queries** targeting likely table/column names:

```
User question: "Show me all returns from enterprise customers this quarter"

LLM-generated search queries:
1. "sales order returns negative value order type KR VBAK"
2. "customer category enterprise large account KNA1 KUNNR"
3. "order creation date current quarter ERDAT filter"
4. "sales document return order header table"
```

- Each of these 3–5 queries is embedded and fired against **Qdrant**.
- Top-3 results per query are merged and deduplicated → typically yields **5–8 candidate tables**.
- This multi-query expansion significantly improved recall for questions spanning multiple SAP modules (SD, MM, FI).

### Stage 3: Self-Refinement — LLM Validates Table Selection

After retrieval, the candidate table DDLs are passed to the LLM with the original question and a focused prompt:

```
You are a SAP SQL expert. The user asked:
"{user_question}"

Here are the candidate tables retrieved:
{candidate_table_ddls}

Are these tables sufficient to answer the question?
- If YES: return only the final list of table names to use.
- If NO: list the additional table names you need.
```

- If the LLM requests additional tables, their DDLs are fetched directly from Qdrant by table name and appended.
- This self-refinement loop runs **once** — in ~**70% of multi-table queries** the LLM identified a missing JOIN table (e.g., a bridge table between customers and sales orgs) that pure vector retrieval had missed.
- After refinement, the confirmed table set is locked and passed to SQL generation.

### Stage 4: SQL Generation

With the final refined table set, a dedicated **SQL Writer LLM call** is made:

```
You are a SAP SQL expert. Write a valid PostgreSQL query to answer:
"{user_question}"

Use only these tables and columns:
{refined_table_ddls_with_sample_rows}

Return:
1. A brief explanation of your approach.
2. The SQL query.
```

- Separating table selection (Stage 3) from SQL generation (Stage 4) into two distinct LLM calls improved SQL accuracy — each call has a single, focused responsibility.
- The LLM also returns a **plain-English explanation** of what the query does, shown to the user as a "reasoning trace" before the results load.

### Stage 5: Execution, Result Preprocessing & Dashboard Generation

SQL is executed via **SQLAlchemy** against the live database. The result set size determines preprocessing:

**If rows ≤ 100:**
- All rows passed directly to the LLM for answer generation.

**If rows > 100:**
Pandas preprocessing kicks in before passing to LLM:
- **Statistical summary per numeric column:** mean, median, mode, min, max, std deviation.
- **Top 50 rows** by relevance (sorted by primary sort column, e.g., date descending or value descending).
- Both the statistical summary and top 50 rows are passed to the LLM — giving it the full picture of the data distribution without blowing the context window.

```python
# Preprocessing for large result sets
df = pd.DataFrame(sql_results)
numeric_cols = df.select_dtypes(include='number').columns
stats = df[numeric_cols].agg(['mean', 'median', 'std', 'min', 'max']).to_dict()
top_50 = df.head(50).to_dict(orient='records')
```

**Dashboard / Chart Generation:**
The same LLM call that generates the answer is also prompted to return **exactly 2 chart specifications**:

```
Based on the data and question, also return 2 charts as JSON:
[
  {"type": "bar", "x": "AUART", "y": "NETWR", "title": "Net Order Value by Order Type"},
  {"type": "line", "x": "ERDAT", "y": "count", "title": "Order Volume Over Time"}
]
```

- The frontend reads these chart specs and renders them using a charting library directly on the dashboard alongside the text answer.
- Asking for exactly 2 charts (not "some charts") was a deliberate prompt engineering decision — it produced consistent, parseable JSON output without over- or under-generation.

### Full System Flow
```
User types business question
        │
        ▼
LLM expands question into 3–5 search queries
        │
        ▼
BGE-M3 embeds each query → Qdrant retrieves top-3 per query
Deduplicate → 5–8 candidate tables
        │
        ▼
[SELF-REFINEMENT]
LLM reviews candidate DDLs + question:
  → Sufficient? Lock table set.
  → Missing table? Fetch by name from Qdrant → append → lock.
        │
        ▼
[SQL GENERATION]
LLM writes SQL using refined DDLs + sample rows
        │
        ▼
SQLAlchemy executes SQL on live database
        │
  ┌─────┴──────┐
≤100 rows   >100 rows
  │               │
  │          Pandas preprocessing:
  │          stats (mean/median/mode/std)
  │          + top 50 rows
  │               │
  └─────┬──────┘
        ▼
LLM generates:
  1. Plain-English answer
  2. 2 chart specs (JSON)
        │
        ▼
Dashboard: Answer + 2 rendered charts
```

### Key Technical Decisions
- **DDL + 5 sample rows per chunk (not DDL alone):** Sample rows gave the LLM real enum values (`AUART = 'TA'`), number formats, and zero-padding patterns — without them, the LLM frequently hallucinated WHERE clause values.
- **LLM query expansion before vector search:** SAP column names (`VBELN`, `KUNNR`) don't appear in natural language questions. Expanding the question into schema-flavored search queries bridged this vocabulary gap.
- **Two-call architecture (table selection → SQL generation):** Combining both into one prompt degraded SQL quality — the model tried to reason about table selection and write SQL simultaneously. Splitting into two focused calls improved accuracy measurably.
- **One self-refinement pass (not a loop):** Running refinement as a loop caused instability — the LLM would sometimes request tables it had just been given. A single refinement pass resolved the majority of missing-table cases cleanly.
- **Preprocessing threshold at 100 rows:** LLM context quality degraded noticeably above ~100 rows of raw data. Statistical summaries + top 50 rows gave better analytical answers than 150+ raw rows.
- **Exactly 2 charts in prompt:** Asking for "relevant charts" produced inconsistent JSON structure. Specifying exactly 2 charts as a structured JSON array made the output reliably parseable for frontend rendering.
- **BGE-M3 over OpenAI embeddings:** On-premise, zero per-query cost, already production-tested at Silver Touch in the main RAG system.
- **Qdrant over Milvus for this use case:** 110 table chunks is a small index — Qdrant's single Docker container deployment was right-sized. Milvus would have been infrastructure overkill.
